question: [File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHashCacheControlHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the `Cache-Control` response header by querying a transaction ID that resolves to a successful `SCHEDULECREATE` transaction (type == `scheduleCreateProtoId` == 42) within `maxScheduledTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs`, causing the server to return `SHORTER_CACHE_CONTROL_HEADER` (`max-age=5`) instead of the default, and by doing so, force downstream caches to serve stale data for only 5 seconds, enabling a timing-based cache poisoning attack?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHashCacheControlHeader()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `transaction.scheduled` check in `getTransactionsByIdOrHashCacheControlHeader()` to force the server to return an empty `{}` cache control header (no caching override) by querying any transaction ID that has a scheduled child transaction, causing the response to bypass CDN caching and hit the origin server on every request, enabling a cache-bypass DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `nonce` query parameter with a value that, when pushed into `params` via `getTransactionByIdQueryParamConditions()`, shifts the `params.lowerConsensusTimestampIndex` and `params.upperConsensusTimestampIndex` offsets, causing the re-query in `getTransactionsByIdOrHash()` to update the wrong parameter index and use an incorrect timestamp bound in the second DB query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByTransactionIdsSql()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction ID where the `validStartTimestamp` is set to `constants.MAX_LONG`, causing `minTimestamp` to remain at `constants.MAX_LONG` and `minTimestamp - config.query.maxValidStartTimestampDriftNs` to underflow, resulting in a negative lower bound that matches all transactions in the database?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByTransactionIdsSql()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `maxConsensusTimestamp` calculation where `timestampField === Transaction.CONSENSUS_TIMESTAMP` causes `transactionKeys[transactionKeys.length - 1]` to be used directly as the upper bound, and by providing a hash that maps to a very recent transaction, cause the upper bound to be set to the current time, scanning the entire recent transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactions()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `timestamp` filter with a very wide range (e.g., `gte:0` and `lte:9999999999.999999999`) combined with a high `limit` value, causing `getTransactionTimestamps()` to execute a full table scan across all transaction, crypto_transfer, token_transfer, and entity_transaction tables simultaneously via the `full outer join` query, exhausting DB resources?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionTimestamps()] Can an unprivileged external user submit multiple `timestamp=eq:X` filters (more than one `eqValues`), causing `timestampRange.eqValues.length > 1` to return `{rows: []}` silently, and by doing so, determine the existence of specific timestamps through timing differences between the short-circuit return and a full DB query, enabling a timing oracle for transaction existence?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: extractSqlFromTransactionsByIdOrHashRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a base64url hash where the decoded bytes, when passed to `getTransactionHash(Buffer.from(transactionIdOrHash, 'base64url'))`, produce a buffer that after `normalizeTransactionHash()` truncation to 32 bytes matches a known Ethereum transaction hash, causing the API to return Hedera transaction details for an Ethereum hash lookup, leaking cross-chain transaction correlation data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction hash (setting `isTransactionHash = true`) combined with a `nonce` or `scheduled` query parameter, and since `acceptedSingleTransactionParameters` only includes `NONCE` and `SCHEDULED`, exploit the fact that these filters are still processed by `getTransactionByIdQueryParamConditions()` even for hash-based lookups, causing unintended filtering behavior that hides valid transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction ID for a batch transaction (where `Transaction.BATCH_KEY` is set), causing `formatTransactionRows()` to call `utils.encodeKey(row.batch_key)` and expose the batch key bytes in the response, leaking cryptographic key material associated with the batch transaction submitter?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: formatTransactionRows()] Can an unprivileged external user query a transaction that has `high_volume = true` and `high_volume_pricing_multiplier` set, and by comparing the `charged_tx_fee` against the `max_fee` and `high_volume_pricing_multiplier` fields returned in `formatTransactionRows()`, reverse-engineer the fee schedule and pricing model to craft transactions that minimize fees in ways not intended by the protocol?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: createCryptoTransferList()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `is_approval: isNil(is_approval) ? false : is_approval` logic in `createCryptoTransferList()` to observe that a transfer has `is_approval = null` in the DB (returned as `false` in the API), masking the true approval status of a transfer and causing downstream integrators to incorrectly classify approved transfers as direct transfers, enabling fraud in systems that rely on this field?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: createTokenTransferList()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the same `is_approval: isNil(is_approval) ? false : is_approval` normalization in `createTokenTransferList()` to cause token transfer approval status to be misrepresented as `false` when the DB value is `null`, leading to incorrect authorization decisions in downstream token management systems that consume the mirror node API?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionTimestampsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an `account.id` filter that, after processing through `buildAndValidateFilters()`, produces an `accountQuery` string containing the substring `ctl.entity_id` literally (e.g., through a specially crafted entity ID format), causing the `.replace(/ctl\.entity_id/g, 'ttl.account_id')` substitution to incorrectly rewrite the token transfer query condition?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionTimestampsQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `nftTransfersUnion` UNION ALL subquery against `EntityTransaction.tableName` by submitting an `accountQuery` that, after the `.replace(/(ctl|t)\./g, '')` prefix stripping, produces a condition that matches entity transactions for accounts other than the requested one, leaking NFT transfer timestamps for unrelated accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction ID that resolves to a transaction with `inner_transactions` containing duplicate entries, causing `rows.push(...innerTransactionRows)` to append duplicate transaction rows to the response, inflating the `transactions` array and causing downstream consumers to process the same transaction multiple times, leading to double-counting of transfers or fees?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction hash that maps to multiple rows in `getTransactionHash()` (hash collision scenario), causing `lookupKeys = rows.map((row) => [payerAccountId, row.consensus_timestamp]).flat()` to produce a large array, and `getTransactionsByTransactionIdsSql()` to generate a SQL query with many OR conditions, causing excessive DB load?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByTransactionIdsSql()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `subqueryConditions` construction where `payerAccountParams` are joined with commas into `PAYER_ACCOUNT_ID in (${payerAccountParams.join(',')})`, and by triggering a hash lookup that returns many rows with different consensus timestamps but the same payer, cause the subquery to scan all crypto_transfer and token_transfer rows for that payer within the timestamp range?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactions()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `transaction.type` filter with a value that maps to a proto ID corresponding to a privileged system transaction type (e.g., node stake update, freeze), causing `getTransactionTimestampsQuery()` to return system-level transactions that expose internal network state or validator information not intended for public consumption?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionTimestamps()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit `bindTimestampRange()` by submitting a request with no timestamp filter, causing the system to automatically bind a default timestamp range, and by timing the response latency, infer the current consensus timestamp of the network, enabling precise timing attacks against time-sensitive transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: doGetTransactions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the pagination link generation in `utils.getPaginationLink()` by observing the `next` link timestamp in the response, which reveals the exact `consensus_timestamp` of the last transaction in the result set, enabling the attacker to enumerate all transactions in the system by following pagination links without any rate limiting?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction ID where `TransactionId.fromString()` parses a `validStartNanos` of `999999999` (maximum), causing `getValidStartNs()` to produce a timestamp string that, when converted to `BigInt`, is near the maximum safe integer value, and combined with `maxTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs`, overflows the upper bound calculation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: extractSqlFromTransactionsByIdOrHashRequest()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a 64-character hex string (without `0x` prefix) that `isValidTransactionHash()` accepts as valid, but which when decoded via `Buffer.from(transactionIdOrHash, 'hex')` produces a 32-byte buffer that matches the truncated prefix of a 48-byte Hedera hash, causing `getTransactionHash()` to return the full 48-byte hash transaction and then the `rows.filter((row) => row.hash.equals(hash))` check in `normalizeTransactionHash` to fail, resulting in an empty result set that throws `NotFoundError` and leaks the existence of the longer hash?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a `scheduled=true` query parameter for a transaction ID that has no scheduled counterpart, causing `mayMissLongTermScheduledTransaction()` to return `true` (via `scheduled && transactions.length === 0`), triggering a second DB query with an extended timestamp range, and by doing this repeatedly, cause amplified DB load (2x queries per request) for any non-existent scheduled transaction ID?

[File: 'File Name: rest/transactions.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getTransactionsByIdOrHash()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `params[params.upperConsensusTimestampIndex]` mutation in the re-query path, where `params[params.lowerConsensusTimestampIndex] + maxScheduledTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs` is computed, to cause the second query to use a different upper bound than intended if `params.lowerConsensusTimestampIndex` was set incorrectly due to the `nonce` or `scheduled` filter shifting the params array?
